responses.text: The New York Post article reports on claims made by Luis Elizondo, who asserts he led the Pentagon's Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program (AATIP) and that the U.S. government is preparing to share information about the 'reality' of UFOs.

The article correctly notes that the December 2020 government funding bill included a mandate for the Director of National Intelligence and the Secretary of Defense to produce an unclassified report on unidentified aerial phenomena (UAP).
That highly anticipated preliminary assessment on UAPs was subsequently released in June 2021 by the Office of the Director of National Intelligence.

Independent sources verify that the AATIP program indeed existed, received a $22 million appropriation advanced by former Senator Harry Reid, and that the Pentagon released authentic videos of UAPs encountered by Navy pilots.

However, there is conflicting evidence regarding Elizondo's specific status; a Pentagon spokesperson denied in 2019 that Elizondo had any responsibilities in the AATIP program, although Senator Harry Reid later defended Elizondo's leadership role.

Furthermore, Elizondo's extraordinary claims about UAPs demonstrating 'transmedium travel,' 11,000 mph speeds, and the ability to disable U.S. nuclear capabilities remain his own allegations rather than scientifically verified facts or official Pentagon conclusions.

The official 2021 ODNI report ultimately concluded that most of the 144 UAP incidents analyzed remain unexplained due to a lack of high-quality data, emphasizing national security and flight safety concerns without concluding the advanced objects were extraterrestrial.

In short, while the article successfully relays that the government has formalized its investigation of unexplained aerial phenomena, it blends official admissions with Elizondo's unverified interpretations of physics-defying aircraft.
